caption: Figure 1. Optimization of hot rolling condition: a) hot-rolled plates according to soaking temperatures, b) SEM micrographs of hot-rolled specimen after soaking at 1150 °C showing intergranular crack, c) SEM micrographs of hot-rolled specimen after soaking at 1100 °C, d) EBSD phase map of hot-rolled specimen after soaking at ${ \sf 1 7 5 0 ^ { \circ } C } ,$ and e) EBSD phase map of hot-rolled specimen after soaking at $1 1 0 0 ^ { \circ } \mathsf C .$ .
paper_abstract: An ultralightweight Fe–30Mn–13.2Al–1.6C–5Cr steel, which contains more than 13 wt% of Al and thereby reduces the density by 20%, is developed. The ultralightweight steel, which is very brittle due to high Al content, is fabricated by optimizing hot rolling and heat treatment conditions. Hot rolling is conducted after soaking at the temperature range of 1100–1200 °C for 2 h. The ultralightweight steel is hot-rolled successfully after soaking at 1100 °C, whereas specimens soaked at 1150 and 1200 °C are intergranularly cracked after hot rolling, resulting from coarse grain and a large fraction and size of ferrite, which is transformed to ordered ${ \mathsf { D O } } _ { 3 }$ phase during cooling, at the grain boundaries. In homogenization heat treatment, water quenching and air cooling are performed, respectively, after holding at $1 0 5 0 ^ { \circ } C$ for 2 h. The air-cooled steel has inferior tensile property due to the formation of brittle ordered ${ \mathsf { D O } } _ { 3 }$ phase at grain boundaries. Meanwhile, the water-quenched steel shows an excellent tensile property, which is attributed to a uniform microstructure comprising austenite, fine κ-carbide in austenite, and a very small fraction of the ordered ${ \mathsf { D O } } _ { 3 }$ phase.
paper_title: Optimization of Process Conditions for Ultralightweight Steel with More Than 13 wt% of Al and 5 wt% of Cr
citation_sentences: shows the hot-rolled plate according to the soaking Figure 1temperature. Both plates soaked at 1200 and 1150 °C had crack after hot rolling, while the plate soaked at 1100 °C was hot-rolled successfully without cracks (Figure 1a). The microstructure and crack in the 1150 °C plate were investigated with scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and electron backscattered diffraction

(EBSD), as shown in Figure 1b,d, respectively. It was found that the microstructure of the $1 1 5 0 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ plate was austenite (face centered cubic (FCC) phase) with large grain size and ferrite (body centered cubic (BCC) phase) at grain boundaries. The BCC phase is identified as ordered $\mathrm { D O } _ { 3 }$ phase by X-ray diffraction (XRD) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) analysis, as shown in Figure 3. The austenite grain size was approximately 60 μm and the average area fraction and thickness of $\mathrm { D O } _ { 3 }$ phase were 11.5% and 5.5 μm, respectively. Interestingly, there is no coarse κ-carbide at grain boundaries in the investigated steel in spite of 13.2 wt% of Al content. It is confirmed that 5 wt% of Cr is effective to prevent precipitation of coarse κ-carbide. The crack of the $1 1 5 0 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ plate was intergranular fracture along austenite grain boundaries. On the other hand, the plate soaked at $1 1 0 0 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ shows relatively fine austenite grain size and small fraction and size of $\mathrm { D O } _ { 3 }$ at grain boundaries (Figure 1c,e). The austenite grain size was 35 μm, and the average area fraction and thickness of $\mathrm { D O } _ { 3 }$ phase were 8.1% and 2.8 μm, respectively. The critical difference between the $1 1 5 0 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ plate and the $1 1 0 0 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ plate is austenite grain size and fraction and size of $\mathrm { D O } _ { 3 }$ at grain boundaries. According to the calculated phase diagram of Fe–Mn–Al–C steels in the previous result[27] reporting that BCC phase exists at the soaking temperature between 1100 and $1 2 0 0 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ and the fraction of BCC phase increases with increasing the soaking temperature, it is expected that the microstructure at soaking temperature is austenite and some ferrite at grain boundaries, which is transformed to ordered $\mathrm { D O } _ { 3 }$ during cooling. Therefore, the plate soaked at higher temperature has a large size of austenite and relatively

citation_sentences: The hot-rolled plate soaked at $1 1 0 0 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ was heat-treated at $1 0 5 0 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ for 2 h, followed by air cooling and water quenching, respectively, as shown in a. SEM micrographs and Figure 2EBSD results of the air-cooled and water-quenched specimens were presented in Figure 2b–e. Austenite and coarse $\mathrm { D O } _ { 3 }$ phase at the grain boundaries were observed in the air-cooled specimen (Figure 2b,d), and the average area fraction and thickness of $\mathrm { \Delta D O } _ { 3 }$ phase were 4.2% and 2.6 μm, respectively, whereas uniform microstructure with austenite and small amount of $\mathrm { D O } _ { 3 }$ was observed in the water-quenched specimen (Figure $^ { 2 \mathrm { c } , \mathrm { e } ) } \mathrm { : }$ , and the average area fraction and thickness of $\mathrm { D O } _ { 3 }$ phase were 1.27% and 2.4 μm, respectively. It was found that transformation of austenite to ferrite (or $\mathrm { D O } _ { 3 }$ phase) happened at a slow cooling rate, so fast cooling is necessary to have uniform microstructure by preventing ferrite transformation.

caption: Figure 3. In-depth identification of phases in water-quenched specimen: a) XRD result and b) TEM results.

citation_sentences: In-depth microstructural analysis on water-quenched specimen was conducted using XRD and TEM, as shown in , which illustrates that the water-quenched specimen Figure 3has austenite and fine κ-carbide inside austenite matrix and BCC phase at grain boundaries was identified as ordered $\mathrm { D O } _ { 3 }$ phase. Finally, stress–strain curves of the air-cooled and waterquenched specimens were illustrated, as shown in . Figure 4The air-cooled specimen shows very poor tensile property. Tensile specimen was fractured at elastic region and even failed

to have yield and tensile strength, resulting from brittle ordered $\mathrm { D O } _ { 3 }$ at grain boundaries. However, the water-quenched specimen shows excellent tensile property with 1048 MPa of tensile strength and 38% of total elongation due to uniform microstructure with austenite and fine κ-carbide inside austenite matrix and very small fraction of $\mathrm { D O } _ { 3 }$ phase. The cross section beneath fractured surface after tensile test was investigated to analyze the difference in tensile behavior between air-cooled and water-quenched specimens and the result was shown in . In the air-cooled specimen, the cracks initiated and Figure 5propagated along $\mathrm { D O } _ { 3 }$ phase at grain boundary and austenite matrix seems not to be plastically deformed, whereas in the water-quenched specimen, austenite matrix was considerably elongated and fractured ductile, which are consistent with stress–strain curves in Figure 4, respectively.

caption: Figure 5. SEM micrographs of the cross section beneath fractured surface after tensile test: a) air-cooled specimen and b) water-quenched specimen.
